title: UI Animation Principles — Purposeful Motion Design
content: UI animation should communicate, not decorate. Easing: ease-out (decelerating — entering elements), ease-in (accelerating — exiting elements), ease-in-out (moving between positions), linear (only for continuous animations like progress bars). Duration: micro 50–100ms (button states, toggles), short 150–250ms (tooltips, fades, small movements), medium 250–400ms (page transitions, modals), long 400–700ms (complex choreography). Motion principles: Purposeful (every animation communicates something), Quick (faster is almost always better in UI), Natural (follow physics — acceleration, deceleration), Choreographed (related elements move in coordinated sequence), Interruptible (animations can be cancelled mid-flight). Stagger related items by 30–50ms each, lead with the most important element, limit total sequence to under 700ms. Always support prefers-reduced-motion. Test on low-powered devices. Use transform/will-change for GPU-accelerated performance.

title: Error Handling UX — Prevention, Detection, Recovery
content: Error handling follows a hierarchy: 1) Prevention — inline validation before submission, smart defaults, confirmation dialogs for destructive actions, constraint-based inputs (date pickers, dropdowns), auto-save. 2) Detection — real-time field validation, form-level validation on submit, network error detection, timeout handling, permission checks. 3) Communication — clear human language (not error codes), explain what happened and why, tell the user what to do next, place messages near the source, use appropriate severity. 4) Recovery — preserve user input (never clear forms on error), offer retry for transient failures, provide alternative paths, auto-retry with backoff for network errors, undo for accidental actions. Error message format: what happened + why (if helpful) + what to do. Context-specific patterns: forms (inline per-field + summary), pages (full-page with retry/back), network (toast/banner with retry), empty results (helpful empty state), permissions (explain access needed). Never blame the user. Test error paths as thoroughly as happy paths.

title: System Feedback Patterns — Keeping Users Informed
content: Every user action deserves acknowledgement. Feedback types: Immediate (button state change, inline validation, toggle response, drag position update), Confirmation (success toast/snackbar, checkmark animation, summary of action, undo option), Status (progress indicators, status badges, activity indicators, system health), Notification (in-app events, badge counts, banners, push notifications). Feedback channels: Visual (colour change, icon, animation, badge), Text (toast, inline text, status label), Audio (click, chime, alert tone), Haptic (tap feedback, success vibration, warning buzz). Hierarchy: 1) inline/contextual — closest to action (preferred), 2) component-level, 3) page-level — banner or toast, 4) system-level — notification outside current view. Duration: toasts auto-dismiss 3–5 seconds, errors persist until resolved, confirmations display briefly with undo window. Match feedback intensity to action importance. Provide undo rather than 'Are you sure?' dialogs. Ensure feedback is accessible — never colour-only.

title: Gesture Patterns — Touch and Pointer Interaction Design
content: Core gestures: Tap (select, activate), Double tap (zoom, like), Long press (context menu, reorder, preview), Swipe (navigate, dismiss, reveal actions), Pinch (zoom), Rotate (maps, images), Drag (move, reorder, adjust), Pull (refresh). Discoverability rules: pair gestures with visible affordances, provide visual hints on first use, always have a non-gesture alternative (button/menu). Feedback: immediate visual response when gesture starts, progress indication during, threshold indicators (snap points, rubber-banding), completion confirmation. Thresholds: minimum 10–15px before activation, velocity thresholds for flick/swipe, direction lock after initial movement, cancel zone (return to start to abort). Conflict resolution: scroll vs swipe uses direction lock, tap vs long press uses 500ms threshold, pinch vs drag uses touch point count, system gestures take priority. Accessibility: every gesture must have a non-gesture alternative, support switch and voice control, respect reduced-motion preferences.

title: Loading States — Perceived Performance Design
content: Loading patterns: Skeleton screens (show layout shape before content, animate with subtle shimmer — use for known content structures), Spinner/Progress (indeterminate for unknown duration, determinate when measurable, keep spinners small), Progressive loading (critical content first, lazy-load below-fold, blur-up images), Optimistic UI (show expected result immediately, reconcile with server, roll back on failure), Placeholder content (realistic proportions, smooth transition to real content). Duration guidelines: under 100ms — no indicator needed, 100ms–1s — subtle indicator (opacity, skeleton), 1–10s — clear loading with progress if possible, over 10s — detailed progress, time estimate, background option. Transitions: fade content in (don't pop), stagger list items 30–50ms, avoid layout shifts on load, maintain scroll position on refresh. Never show a blank screen. Match skeleton shapes to actual content. Avoid competing loading indicators. Test on slow connections. Respect reduced-motion for shimmer animations.

title: Micro-Interaction Specification — Trigger, Rules, Feedback, Loops
content: Micro-interactions make interfaces feel alive. Dan Saffer's four-part framework: 1) Trigger — what initiates it: user action (click, hover, swipe), system event (notification, completion), or conditional (time-based, threshold). 2) Rules — what happens: logic, sequence, conditions, branching. 3) Feedback — how the user perceives it: visual (colour, size, position), motion (animation, transition), audio (click, chime), haptic (vibration). 4) Loops and Modes — does it repeat? Does it change over time? First-time vs repeat behaviour, progressive disclosure. Common micro-interactions: toggle with state animation, pull-to-refresh with progress, like/favourite with celebration, form validation with inline feedback, button press with depth/scale, swipe with threshold feedback, long-press with radial progress. Specification format: name, trigger, rules (sequence), feedback (visual/audio/haptic), duration/easing, loop behaviour, accessibility notes. Keep durations 100–500ms. Every micro-interaction needs a purpose.
source: Dan Saffer — Microinteractions: Designing with Details

title: State Machines — Modelling Predictable UI Behaviour
content: State machines model complex UI as finite states with defined transitions, eliminating impossible states. Components: States (distinct modes — idle, loading, success, error), Events (triggers — click, submit, timeout, response), Transitions (rules — on event X in state A, go to state B), Actions (side effects — fetch data, show toast, log), Guards (conditions — isValid, hasPermission). Common patterns: Form (idle → editing → validating → submitting → success/error → idle), Data fetching (idle → loading → success/error, error → retrying), Authentication (logged-out → authenticating → logged-in → logging-out), Multi-step wizard (step1 → step2 → ... → review → submitting → complete). Modelling approach: list all states, list all events, define valid transitions, identify impossible states to prevent, add guards, define entry/exit actions. Benefits: eliminates impossible states (no loading + error simultaneously), makes edge cases visible, shared language between design and engineering, testable specification. Every state should have a way out — no dead ends.
